title: Topic 134: Why Players Buy Multiple Copies: The Collector's Curse
content:
This paper delves into the perplexing phenomenon of video game enthusiasts purchasing multiple copies of the same game, a behavior we have dubbed "The Collector's Curse." While seemingly irrational to the uninitiated, this practice is a fascinating intersection of consumer behavior, psychological drivers, and the unique culture of gaming. We explore the motivations behind this seemingly redundant accumulation, from the intoxicating allure of sealed-box preservation to the speculative frenzy of the collector's market. Through a synthesis of existing literature on collecting, coupled with a healthy dose of witty observation, this paper argues that the act of buying the same game twice (or thrice, or more) is not mere madness, but a complex and often contradictory expression of passion, identity, and the eternal human quest for control in a world of digital ephemera. We also briefly touch on the existential dread of a pristine, shrink-wrapped game, forever trapped in a state of potential, a digital ghost in the machine that will never be played. Is it a collection, or a cry for help? We're not sure, but we have three copies of this paper, just in case.

## INTRODUCTION

The digital age was supposed to liberate us from the tyranny of physical media. We were promised a future of ethereal data, a world where our entertainment would stream effortlessly from the cloud, unburdened by the clutter of plastic boxes and fragile discs. And yet, a curious thing has happened. A significant and growing subculture of video game players has not only clung to the tangible artifacts of their hobby but has taken this devotion to a new, and some might say, absurd, extreme: they are buying multiple copies of the same game. This is not a case of a faulty disc or a lost cartridge. This is a deliberate, conscious decision to own two, three, or even a dozen identical copies of a single title. 

What drives a seemingly rational individual to purchase a second copy of a game they already own, a game they may have already completed? Is it a misguided attempt to corner the market on a limited-run indie title? A Freudian obsession with possessing the 

## LITERATURE REVIEW

While the specific phenomenon of multi-copy game collecting is a relatively new area of academic inquiry (read: we're making it up as we go), a wealth of research exists on the broader psychology of collecting. This body of work provides a solid, if slightly dusty, foundation for understanding the Collector's Curse. Early psychoanalytic theories, for instance, posited that collecting is a manifestation of unresolved childhood conflicts, with Freud himself suggesting a link to anal-retentive tendencies. While we're not suggesting that every gamer with a shelf full of sealed copies of 'Chrono Trigger' is sublimating a potty-training trauma, the idea that collecting fulfills a deep-seated psychological need is a recurring theme. More contemporary research has moved beyond the Freudian couch and into the realm of cognitive and social psychology. Studies on consumer behavior, for instance, highlight the role of 'perceived scarcity' and the 'endowment effect' in driving collecting behavior. The knowledge that a particular edition of a game is 'limited' can trigger a powerful fear of missing out (FOMO), compelling consumers to purchase it, even if they have no immediate intention of playing it. The endowment effect, in which we place a higher value on things we own, further reinforces this behavior. Once a collector has one copy, the desire to protect and even enhance their 'investment' can lead to the acquisition of more. Research from the Eller College of Management at the University of Arizona, for example, suggests that collecting is a way of imposing order on a chaotic world, a 'timeless endeavor to make sense of our environment.' In a world of fleeting digital downloads and server shutdowns, a physical collection of games can feel like a tangible and permanent achievement. The act of organizing, cataloging, and displaying these collections can provide a powerful sense of control and mastery, a feeling that is often lacking in the unpredictable world of online gaming.

## ANALYSIS

So, what is actually going on in the mind of a multi-copy collector? Is it a simple case of consumerism gone wild, or is there something deeper at play? We propose a multi-faceted model, a 'Grand Unified Theory of Redundant Game Ownership,' if you will. This model is built on three pillars: the 'Digital Doomsday Prepper,' the 'Speculator,' and the 'Connoisseur.'

The Digital Doomsday Prepper is motivated by a deep-seated anxiety about the impermanence of digital media. They have seen their favorite digital games disappear from online stores, their online services shut down, and their save files corrupted. For them, a physical copy is a bulwark against the inevitable digital apocalypse. A second, sealed copy is an even better insurance policy, a pristine artifact that can be lovingly preserved for a future in which the internet is a distant memory and the only way to play 'The Last of Us' is on a scavenged PlayStation 4 powered by a hand-crank generator. This behavior is a rational response to a very real problem. The video game industry has a notoriously poor track record when it comes to preservation, and for many players, the only way to guarantee access to their favorite games is to own them outright, in multiple, redundant formats.

The Speculator, on the other hand, is driven by a different kind of motivation: profit. They have seen the prices of rare and out-of-print games skyrocket on the secondary market, and they are hoping to cash in on the next big thing. They buy multiple copies of limited-edition releases, not to play them, but to hoard them, waiting for the day when they can sell them for a handsome profit. This behavior is not unique to video games, of course. It can be seen in the world of comic books, sneakers, and even Beanie Babies. But the video game market, with its rapid-fire release schedule and its constant churn of new consoles and technologies, is particularly fertile ground for this kind of speculation.

Finally, we have the Connoisseur. This is the collector who is not just interested in owning the game, but in owning the *perfect* version of the game. They will buy multiple copies in search of the one with the pristine, unblemished box, the perfectly centered disc, and the crisp, uncreased manual. They are the true aesthetes of the collecting world, the ones who appreciate the game not just as a piece of software, but as a physical object, a work of art. They are the ones who will pay a premium for the Japanese version of a game, simply because the cover art is better. They are the ones who will hunt down the 'black label' version of a PlayStation game, because they know that it is the original, unadulterated release. For the Connoisseur, collecting is not just a hobby, it is a passion, a calling, a way of life.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The Collector's Curse is not just a theoretical construct; it is a very real phenomenon, with countless examples to be found in the wild. One need only browse the forums of a site like Reddit's r/gamecollecting to find a treasure trove of anecdotal evidence. Here, you will find posts from collectors proudly displaying their multiple copies of the same game, often with detailed explanations of the subtle variations between each version. You will find debates raging over the relative merits of the 'black label' versus the 'greatest hits' version of a game. You will find tales of collectors who have spent years, and small fortunes, tracking down every last variant of their favorite title.

But the Collector's Curse is not just limited to the world of online forums. It has also had a very real impact on the video game market. The speculative frenzy surrounding rare and limited-edition games has driven prices to astronomical heights. In 2021, a sealed copy of 'Super Mario Bros.' for the Nintendo Entertainment System sold for a staggering $2 million. This was not a one-off event. In the same year, a sealed copy of 'The Legend of Zelda' sold for $870,000. These sales, and others like them, have created a feedback loop, in which the high prices of rare games encourage more speculation, which in turn drives prices even higher. This has led to a situation in which many collectors are priced out of the market, unable to afford the very games they love.

Of course, not all multi-copy collectors are motivated by profit. For many, the act of collecting is a labor of love, a way of paying homage to the games that have shaped their lives. Take, for example, the collector who owns multiple copies of 'Silent Hill 2'. They may have the original PlayStation 2 release, the 'Greatest Hits' version, the Japanese release with its different cover art, and the high-definition remaster for the PlayStation 3. Each of these versions offers a slightly different experience, a slightly different window into the world of the game. For the true connoisseur, these subtle differences are not just interesting, they are essential.

## DISCUSSION

The Collector's Curse, in all its multi-faceted glory, offers a fascinating window into the human psyche. It is a testament to our enduring fascination with objects, our desire for control in an increasingly chaotic world, and our seemingly infinite capacity for self-deception. It is a reminder that, even in the digital age, we are still very much attached to the physical world. We still crave the satisfaction of holding a tangible object in our hands, of owning something that is real and permanent.

But the Collector's Curse also raises some troubling questions. At what point does a healthy hobby become an unhealthy obsession? At what point does a passion for games become a compulsion to consume? The line between collecting and hoarding can be a fine one, and it is a line that many collectors have crossed without even realizing it. The pursuit of a complete collection can be an all-consuming one, leading to financial hardship, social isolation, and a whole host of other problems. It is a classic case of the tail wagging the dog, of the collection taking over the life of the collector.

Furthermore, the speculative frenzy surrounding rare games has had a corrosive effect on the collecting community. It has turned a hobby that was once about passion and nostalgia into a high-stakes game of financial speculation. It has created a culture of haves and have-nots, in which the wealthy are able to hoard the most desirable games, while the less fortunate are left to pick over the scraps. This is not a healthy state of affairs, and it is one that threatens the very future of the hobby.

## CONCLUSION

In the end, the Collector's Curse is a deeply personal and often contradictory phenomenon. It is a reflection of our hopes, our fears, and our desires, all played out on the grand stage of the video game market. It is a testament to the enduring power of physical media in an increasingly digital world. And it is a reminder that, for some people, one copy is never enough.

So, the next time you see a collector proudly displaying their multiple copies of the same game, do not be too quick to judge. They may be a Digital Doomsday Prepper, a Speculator, or a Connoisseur. They may be a victim of FOMO, a slave to the endowment effect, or a connoisseur of the highest order. Or they may just be a little bit crazy. But whatever their motivation, they are a part of a fascinating and often misunderstood subculture, a subculture that is worthy of our attention, our respect, and, perhaps, our pity.
references:
Cao, C. C., Reimann, M., & Brucks, M. (2025). Seeking Structure in Collections: Desire for Control Motivates Engagement in Collecting. *Journal of Consumer Research*, 52(3), 480-497.

Formanek, R. (1994). Why they collect: Collectors reveal their motivations. *Interpreting objects and collections*, 339-347.

Nordsletten, A. E., & Mataix-Cols, D. (2012). Hoarding versus collecting: Where does pathology diverge from play?. *Clinical psychology review*, 32(3), 165-176.

Pearce, S. M. (1994). *Interpreting objects and collections*. Routledge.

Toups, Z. O., Crenshaw, N. K., Wehbe, R. R., Tondello, G. F., & Nacke, L. E. (2016, October). The collecting itself feels good. In *Proceedings of the 2016 annual symposium on computer-human interaction in play* (pp. 276-290).
